metadata.summary: Observe real users to discover what works, what confuses, and where they struggle. Generates test plans, scripts, and severity-rated findings reports.

content.title: Usability Testing
content.summary: Observe real users completing tasks with a product to discover what works, what confuses, and where users struggle. Generates test plans, session scripts, and structured findings reports with severity ratings. Use when you need evidence-based validation of design decisions before or after launch.

why.p: Design assumptions fail silently until users interact with the actual interface. Five participants in a moderated session reliably surface 85% of critical usability problems (Nielsen's heuristic), catching issues before development cost compounds. Severity-rated findings give engineering a prioritized backlog rather than a wishlist.

list.item: A feature or flow has never been tested with real users
Conversion or task-completion metrics show unexplained drops
A redesign is complete and ready for pre-launch validation
Synthesizing recordings or transcripts into structured findings for stakeholders
Generating test plans from a feature spec or acceptance criteria set
Nothing testable yet (no wireframe or prototype) — nothing to observe
Fewer than 3 participants available — no pattern detection is meaningful at that scale
Large-N quantitative benchmarking (SUS scoring, statistical significance) — use survey instruments instead
As a substitute for live facilitation — agents cannot observe non-verbal cues or probe nuance

how-to.step: Define Goals — State what you want to learn before designing tasks or recruiting participants. Goals determine task design, participant criteria, and what metrics to collect. Without clear goals, sessions drift and findings are unfocused. Goal examples: Can users complete checkout in under 3 minutes? Do users understand the navigation structure? What blocks users from signing up?

Create Test Plan — Document objectives, participant profile, tasks, metrics, and test environment before scheduling any sessions. A written plan aligns stakeholders, prevents scope creep during sessions, and creates an audit trail for findings.

Write Tasks — Write tasks as realistic scenarios with a clear goal and no hints about the solution path. Task wording that hints at the solution (mentioning button names or menu paths) invalidates the test by guiding the user to the answer. Scenario: You need to change your delivery address for an upcoming order. Task: Find your recent order and update the shipping address. Success: User locates order and changes address. Avoid: "Click the account button and select orders" — gives away the answer. Use "Find your order history" instead.

Recruit Participants — Recruit 5-8 participants per user segment who match the target user profile. Nielsen's research shows 5 participants reveal 85% of usability problems. Below 3, pattern detection is meaningless. Mix experience levels to surface both novice and expert issues.

Conduct Sessions — Follow a consistent session structure: intro (5 min), pre-test questions (5 min), tasks (30-45 min), post-test questions (10 min), debrief (5 min). Consistent structure enables cross-session comparison and ensures participants understand the think-aloud protocol before tasks begin. Do not help participants during tasks, even when they are visibly struggling. Helping invalidates the observation. Struggling is data — it shows where the design fails.

Analyze Results — Group observations by task, rate severity by frequency and task-blocking impact, then prioritize findings. Severity rating directs engineering effort to problems with the highest user impact before those that are merely annoying.

Report and Act — Each finding must include: severity, affected task, frequency (N of total participants), observed problem, evidence, and a concrete recommendation. Findings without evidence are dismissed. Findings without recommendations create discussion but no action.

rule.statement: Tasks must be scenario-based, not instruction-based: describe a situation and goal, never name UI elements or describe navigation steps.
rule.rationale: Instruction-based tasks ("click the account button") test whether users can follow directions, not whether the design is discoverable. Scenario tasks ("you need to update your address") test the actual usability of the design.

rule.statement: Each task must have a defined success criterion before the session begins.
rule.rationale: Without pre-defined success criteria, observers disagree on whether the user succeeded, making severity rating unreliable and findings non-comparable across sessions.

example.note: Scenario: You need to change your delivery address for an upcoming order. Task: Find your recent order and update the shipping address. Success: User locates order and changes address without facilitator help.
"Click the account button and select orders" — gives away the solution path, invalidating the observation of whether users can discover it themselves.
"Find the settings" — too vague. No success criterion. Different observers will disagree on success.

rule.statement: Never help participants during tasks, even when they are clearly stuck and frustrated.
rule.rationale: Every moment of struggle is data showing where the design fails. Helping invalidates both that observation and the participant's remaining task data.

rule.statement: Separate observation (what happened) from inference (why it happened) in session notes.
rule.rationale: Observers who record inferences contaminate data. Write "user clicked Help three times before finding Settings" not "user was confused by the navigation." Agents can infer later from clean observation data.

rule.statement: Rate every finding on a four-level severity scale before prioritizing recommendations.
rule.rationale: Without severity rating, teams treat all findings equally, spending effort on minor friction while critical blockers remain unfixed.

list.item: Critical: user cannot complete the task — fix before any release
High: significant difficulty completing the task — fix before launch
Medium: some struggle but task completed — fix soon after launch
Low: minor friction, task completed easily — fix when possible

rule.statement: Use participant count and task-blocking status together to assign severity: blocked AND 60%+ of participants = Critical; blocked OR 60%+ = High; 40-60% = Medium; below 40% = Low.
rule.rationale: Frequency data prevents over-weighting verbally expressive participants whose observations sound severe but are idiosyncratic.

example.note: Finding: Checkout "Place Order" button not visible without scrolling on mobile. Severity: Critical (7 of 8 participants failed to complete checkout without help). Task: Purchase a product. Frequency: 7/8 participants. Problem: Button below fold on 375px viewport. Evidence: P1 "I don't know how to finish", P3 tapped Back three times. Recommendation: Fix button position above fold on mobile, or add sticky CTA bar.

list.item: Moderated: facilitator present — use for complex tasks that need probing and follow-up questions
Unmoderated: remote automated — use for scale, quick validation, and metric collection
Think-aloud: user verbalizes thoughts — use to understand mental models and decision-making
First-click: where users click first — use to test navigation and information architecture

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Agent reads a feature spec or design document and generates a complete test plan (objectives, tasks, metrics, session script). After sessions are conducted by a human facilitator, the agent receives session notes and synthesizes findings: grouping observations by task, rating severity, drafting recommendations. Final pass produces executive summary and ranked action list.

rule.statement: When using agents to generate tasks, always review agent-generated task wording before use. LLMs default to instructional framing that invalidates tasks.
rule.rationale: Agents asked to generate tasks will sometimes include solution hints ("click the settings icon"). This corrupts the validity of the test.

rule.statement: Human-in-the-loop required for Critical finding classification before backlog entry. Stakes are too high for unreviewed AI judgment on blockers.
rule.rationale: Trusted agent severity ratings for Critical findings require human review first. Agents over-weight verbally expressed confusion.

antipattern.description: Helping participants who are struggling during tasks.
antipattern.reason: Every moment of struggle is data. Helping invalidates the observation and corrupts the participant's remaining session data.

antipattern.description: Recruiting 5 total participants across 3 user segments (fewer than 2 per segment).
antipattern.reason: Pattern detection requires 5 participants per segment, not 5 total. Cross-segment findings are meaningless without sufficient N per segment.

antipattern.description: Reporting only qualitative observations without severity ratings or frequency counts.
antipattern.reason: Unrated findings give engineering no basis for prioritization. All findings appear equally important, delaying critical fixes while teams debate minor ones.

antipattern.description: Synthesis agents trusting session notes without ground truth video timestamps.
antipattern.reason: Findings reports generated without ground truth video timestamps are hard for teams to verify and may be dismissed.

list.item: Write tasks as scenarios, not instructions — "You need to update your shipping address" beats "Go to Account Settings"
Pilot the test with one internal participant before the real sessions; catches broken prototypes and unclear tasks
Record sessions even for unmoderated tests — raw video is the audit trail when stakeholders dispute findings
Aim for 5 participants per distinct user segment, not 5 total across segments
Separate observation (what happened) from inference (why it happened) in notes; agents can infer later from clean observation data
Prioritize findings by severity before effort — a Critical finding that takes 30 minutes to fix outranks a Low finding requiring a redesign

template.purpose: Full test plan template: objectives, methodology, tasks, metrics, schedule
Facilitator script: intro, pre-test questions, tasks, post-test debrief
Finding format: severity, frequency, evidence, recommendation
LLM prompt for synthesizing session notes into a prioritized findings report
LLM prompt for generating a test plan from a feature spec
